infobox.value: and colloquially the **Tories**,[[17]](#cite_note-17) is one of the two main political parties in the United Kingdom, along with the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"). It sits on the [centre-right](/wiki/Centre-right_politics "Centre-right politics")[[18]](#cite_note-inac-18) to [right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing_politics "Right-wing politics")[[21]](#cite_note-accu-21) of the [left–right political spectrum](/wiki/Left%E2%80%93right_political_spectrum "Left–right political spectrum"). Following its defeat by Labour at the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election") it is currently the second-largest party by the number of votes cast and number of seats in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"); as such it has the formal parliamentary role of [His Majesty's Most Loyal Opposition](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Most_Loyal_Opposition "His Majesty's Most Loyal Opposition"). It encompasses various ideological factions including [one-nation conservatives](/wiki/One-nation_conservatism "One-nation conservatism"), [Thatcherites](/wiki/Thatcherism "Thatcherism") and [traditionalist conservatives](/wiki/Traditionalist_conservatism "Traditionalist conservatism"). There have been 20 Conservative [prime ministers](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom"). The party meets annually during Autumn, for the [Conservative Party Conference](/wiki/Conservative_Party_Conference "Conservative Party Conference").

The **Conservative and Unionist Party**, commonly the **Conservative Party** and colloquially the **Tories**,[[17]](#cite_note-17) is one of the two main political parties in the United Kingdom, along with the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"). It sits on the [centre-right](/wiki/Centre-right_politics "Centre-right politics")[[18]](#cite_note-inac-18) to [right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing_politics "Right-wing politics")[[21]](#cite_note-accu-21) of the [left–right political spectrum](/wiki/Left%E2%80%93right_political_spectrum "Left–right political spectrum"). Following its defeat by Labour at the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election") it is currently the second-largest party by the number of votes cast and number of seats in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"); as such it has the formal parliamentary role of [His Majesty's Most Loyal Opposition](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Most_Loyal_Opposition "His Majesty's Most Loyal Opposition"). It encompasses various ideological factions including [one-nation conservatives](/wiki/One-nation_conservatism "One-nation conservatism"), [Thatcherites](/wiki/Thatcherism "Thatcherism") and [traditionalist conservatives](/wiki/Traditionalist_conservatism "Traditionalist conservatism"). There have been 20 Conservative [prime ministers](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom"). The party meets annually during Autumn, for the [Conservative Party Conference](/wiki/Conservative_Party_Conference "Conservative Party Conference").

The Conservative Party was founded in 1834 from the [Tory Party](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)") and was one of two dominant political parties in the 19th century, along with the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)"). Under [Benjamin Disraeli](/wiki/Benjamin_Disraeli "Benjamin Disraeli") it played a preeminent role in politics at the height of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). In 1912 the [Liberal Unionist Party](/wiki/Liberal_Unionist_Party "Liberal Unionist Party") merged with the party to form the Conservative and Unionist Party. Its rivalry with the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") has shaped modern British politics for the last century. [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") sought to modernise the party after [his election as leader in 2005](/wiki/2005_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "2005 Conservative Party leadership election"), and the party governed from 2010 to 2024 under five prime ministers, latterly [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak").

The party has generally adopted [liberal economic policies](/wiki/Economic_liberalism "Economic liberalism") favouring [free markets](/wiki/Free_market "Free market") since the 1980s, although historically it advocated [protectionism](/wiki/Protectionism "Protectionism"). The party is [British unionist](/wiki/Unionism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Unionism in the United Kingdom"), opposing a [united Ireland](/wiki/United_Ireland "United Ireland") as well as [English](/wiki/English_independence "English independence"), [Northern Irish](/wiki/Ulster_nationalism "Ulster nationalism"), [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_independence "Scottish independence") and [Welsh independence](/wiki/Welsh_independence "Welsh independence"), and has been critical of [devolution](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom"). Historically the party supported the continuance and maintenance of the British Empire. The party has taken various approaches towards the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU), with [Eurosceptic](/wiki/Euroscepticism "Euroscepticism") and, to a decreasing extent, [pro-European](/wiki/Pro-Europeanism "Pro-Europeanism") factions within it. Historically the party took a [socially conservative](/wiki/Social_conservatism "Social conservatism") approach.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) In defence policy it supports an [independent nuclear weapons programme](/wiki/Trident_(UK_nuclear_programme) "Trident (UK nuclear programme)") and commitment to [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") membership.

For much of modern British political history the United Kingdom exhibited a wide [urban–rural political divide](/wiki/Urban%E2%80%93rural_political_divide "Urban–rural political divide");[[24]](#cite_note-24) the party's voting and financial [support base](/wiki/Voting_bloc "Voting bloc") has historically consisted primarily of homeowners, business-owners, farmers, real-estate-developers and [middle-class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class") voters, especially in rural and suburban areas of [England](/wiki/England "England").[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) Since [the EU membership referendum in 2016](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") the Conservatives have targeted [working-class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") voters from traditional Labour strongholds.[[30]](#cite_note-The_Independent-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Mueller-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) The party's domination of British politics throughout the 20th century made it one of the most electorally successful political parties in history,[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) although its support has declined in recent years,[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) reaching its lowest vote share ever in the 2024 election.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

## History

Main article: [History of the Conservative Party (UK)](/wiki/History_of_the_Conservative_Party_(UK) "History of the Conservative Party (UK)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Sir_Robert_Peel%2C_2nd_Bt_by_Henry_William_Pickersgill-detail.jpg/250px-Sir_Robert_Peel%2C_2nd_Bt_by_Henry_William_Pickersgill-detail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Robert_Peel,_2nd_Bt_by_Henry_William_Pickersgill-detail.jpg)

[Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel"), twice [Prime Minister of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") and founder of the Conservative Party

### Origins

Some writers trace the party's origins to the [Tory Party](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)"), which it soon replaced. Other historians point to a faction, rooted in the 18th-century [Whig Party](/wiki/Whigs_(British_political_party) "Whigs (British political party)"), that coalesced around [William Pitt the Younger](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger") in the 1780s. They were known as "Independent Whigs", "[Friends of Mr Pitt](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party)#Friends_of_Mr_Pitt "Tories (British political party)")" or "Pittites", and never used terms such as "Tory" or "Conservative". From about 1812 the name "Tory" was commonly used for a new party that, according to the historian Robert Blake, "are the ancestors of Conservatism". Blake adds that Pitt's successors after 1812 "were not in any sense standard-bearers of 'true Toryism'".[[42]](#cite_note-42)

*Tory* was an insult that entered [English politics](/wiki/Politics_of_England "Politics of England") during the [Exclusion Bill](/wiki/Exclusion_Crisis "Exclusion Crisis") crisis of 1678–1681, which derived from the [Middle Irish](/wiki/Middle_Irish "Middle Irish") word *tóraidhe* (modern [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *tóraí*) meaning *[outlaw](/wiki/Outlaw "Outlaw")* or *robber*, which in turn derived from the Irish word *tóir*, meaning *pursuit*, since outlaws were "pursued men".[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The term "[Conservative](/wiki/Conservative "Conservative")" was suggested as a title for the party in an article by [J. Wilson Croker](/wiki/John_Wilson_Croker "John Wilson Croker") published in the *[Quarterly Review](/wiki/Quarterly_Review "Quarterly Review")* in 1830.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The name immediately caught on and was formally adopted under the aegis of [Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel") around 1834. Peel is acknowledged as the founder of the Conservative Party, which he created with the announcement of the [Tamworth Manifesto](/wiki/Tamworth_Manifesto "Tamworth Manifesto"). The term "Conservative Party" rather than Tory was the dominant usage by 1845.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### 1867–1914: Conservatives and Unionists

The widening of the electoral franchise in the 19th century forced the Conservative Party to popularise its approach under [Edward Smith-Stanley, 14th Earl of Derby](/wiki/Edward_Smith-Stanley,_14th_Earl_of_Derby "Edward Smith-Stanley, 14th Earl of Derby"), and [Benjamin Disraeli](/wiki/Benjamin_Disraeli "Benjamin Disraeli"), who carried through their own expansion of the franchise with the [Reform Act 1867](/wiki/Reform_Act_1867 "Reform Act 1867"). The party was initially opposed to further expansion of the electorate but eventually allowed passage of [William Ewart Gladstone](/wiki/William_Ewart_Gladstone "William Ewart Gladstone")'s [Representation of the People Act 1884](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1884 "Representation of the People Act 1884"). In 1886 the party formed an alliance with [Spencer Cavendish](/wiki/Spencer_Cavendish,_8th_Duke_of_Devonshire "Spencer Cavendish, 8th Duke of Devonshire")'s and [Joseph Chamberlain](/wiki/Joseph_Chamberlain "Joseph Chamberlain")'s new [Liberal Unionist Party](/wiki/Liberal_Unionist_Party "Liberal Unionist Party") and, under the statesmen [Robert Gascoyne-Cecil](/wiki/Robert_Gascoyne-Cecil,_3rd_Marquess_of_Salisbury "Robert Gascoyne-Cecil, 3rd Marquess of Salisbury") and [Arthur Balfour](/wiki/Arthur_Balfour "Arthur Balfour"), held power for all but three of the following twenty years before suffering a heavy defeat in [1906](/wiki/1906_United_Kingdom_general_election "1906 United Kingdom general election"), when it split over the issue of [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade").

Young [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") denounced Chamberlain's attack on free trade, and helped to organise the opposition inside the Unionist/Conservative Party. Nevertheless, Balfour, as party leader, introduced protectionist legislation.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Churchill crossed the floor and formally joined the Liberal Party (he rejoined the Conservatives in 1925). In December, Balfour lost control of his party, as the defections multiplied. He was replaced by Liberal Prime Minister [Henry Campbell-Bannerman](/wiki/Henry_Campbell-Bannerman "Henry Campbell-Bannerman") who called [an election in January 1906](/wiki/1906_United_Kingdom_general_election "1906 United Kingdom general election"), which produced a massive Liberal victory. Liberal Prime Minister [H. H. Asquith](/wiki/H._H._Asquith "H. H. Asquith") enacted a great deal of reform legislation, but the Unionists worked hard at grassroots organizing. Two general elections were held in 1910, [in January](/wiki/January_1910_United_Kingdom_general_election "January 1910 United Kingdom general election") and [in December](/wiki/December_1910_United_Kingdom_general_election "December 1910 United Kingdom general election"). The two main parties were now almost dead equal in seats, but the Liberals kept control with a coalition with the [Irish Parliamentary Party](/wiki/Irish_Parliamentary_Party "Irish Parliamentary Party").[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)

In 1912 the Liberal Unionists merged with the Conservative Party. In Ireland the [Irish Unionist Alliance](/wiki/Irish_Unionist_Alliance "Irish Unionist Alliance") had been formed in 1891 which merged Unionists who were opposed to [Irish Home Rule](/wiki/Irish_Home_Rule_movement "Irish Home Rule movement") into one political movement. Its MPs took the Conservative [whip](/wiki/Whip_(politics)#United_Kingdom "Whip (politics)") at Westminster, essentially forming the Irish wing of the party until 1922. In Britain the Conservative Party was known as the Unionist Party because of its opposition to home rule.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) Under [Bonar Law](/wiki/Bonar_Law "Bonar Law")'s leadership in 1911–1914, the party morale improved, the "radical right" wing was contained, and the party machinery strengthened. It made some progress toward developing constructive social policies.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### First World War

Further information: [History of the United Kingdom during the First World War](/wiki/History_of_the_United_Kingdom_during_the_First_World_War "History of the United Kingdom during the First World War")

While the [Liberals](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") were mostly against the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") until the invasion of Belgium, Conservative leaders were strongly in favour of aiding [France](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") and stopping [Germany](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"). The Liberal party was in full control of the government until its mismanagement of the war effort under the [Shell Crisis](/wiki/Shell_Crisis_of_1915 "Shell Crisis of 1915") badly hurt its reputation. An all-party coalition government was formed in May 1915. In late 1916 the Liberal [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George") became prime minister, but the Liberals soon split and the Conservatives dominated the government, especially after their [landslide in the 1918 election](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election "1918 United Kingdom general election"). The Liberal party never recovered, but the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") gained strength after 1920.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Nigel Keohane finds that the Conservatives were bitterly divided before 1914 but the war pulled the party together, allowing it to emphasise patriotism as it found new leadership and worked out its positions on the Irish question, socialism, electoral reform, and the issue of intervention in the economy. The fresh emphasis on [anti-socialism](/wiki/Anti-socialism "Anti-socialism") was its response to the growing strength of the Labour Party. When electoral reform was an issue, it worked to protect their base in rural England.[[55]](#cite_note-55) It aggressively sought female voters in the 1920s, often relying on patriotic themes.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### 1920–1945

Main article: [Interwar Britain](/wiki/Interwar_Britain "Interwar Britain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Sir_Winston_Churchill_-_19086236948.jpg/250px-Sir_Winston_Churchill_-_19086236948.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Winston_Churchill_-_19086236948.jpg)

[Sir Winston Churchill](/wiki/Sir_Winston_Churchill "Sir Winston Churchill"), who was Prime Minister during World War II and in the 1950s

In 1922 Bonar Law and [Stanley Baldwin](/wiki/Stanley_Baldwin "Stanley Baldwin") led the breakup of the coalition, and the Conservatives governed until 1923, when a minority Labour government led by [Ramsay MacDonald](/wiki/Ramsay_MacDonald "Ramsay MacDonald") came to power. The Conservatives regained power in 1924 but were defeated in 1929 as a minority Labour government took office. In 1931, following the collapse of the Labour minority government, it entered another coalition, which was dominated by the Conservatives with some support from factions of both the Liberal Party and the Labour Party ([National Labour](/wiki/National_Labour_Organisation "National Labour Organisation") and [National Liberals](/wiki/National_Liberal_Party_(UK,_1931) "National Liberal Party (UK, 1931)")).[[57]](#cite_note-Vernon_Bogdanor,_Multi-Party_Politics_and_the_Constitution-57) In May 1940, during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), a more balanced coalition was formed[[57]](#cite_note-Vernon_Bogdanor,_Multi-Party_Politics_and_the_Constitution-57)—the [National Government](/wiki/National_Government_(United_Kingdom) "National Government (United Kingdom)")—which, under the leadership of [Sir Winston Churchill](/wiki/Sir_Winston_Churchill "Sir Winston Churchill"), saw the United Kingdom through the Second World War. However, the party lost the [1945 general election](/wiki/1945_United_Kingdom_general_election "1945 United Kingdom general election") in a landslide to the resurgent Labour Party.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

The concept of the "property-owning democracy" was coined by [Noel Skelton](/wiki/Noel_Skelton "Noel Skelton") in 1923 and became a core principle of the party.[[60]](#cite_note-Matthew_Francis_2011-60)

### 1945–1975: Post-war consensus

Main articles: [Post-war consensus](/wiki/Post-war_consensus "Post-war consensus") and [Post-war Britain (1945–1979)](/wiki/Post-war_Britain_(1945%E2%80%931979) "Post-war Britain (1945–1979)")

#### Popular dissatisfaction

While serving in Opposition during the late 1940s, the Conservative Party exploited and incited growing public anger at [food rationing](/wiki/Rationing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Rationing in the United Kingdom"), scarcity, controls, [austerity](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity") and government bureaucracy. It used the dissatisfaction with the [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist") and [egalitarian](/wiki/Egalitarian "Egalitarian") policies of the Labour Party to rally middle-class supporters and build a political comeback that won them the [1951 general election](/wiki/1951_United_Kingdom_general_election "1951 United Kingdom general election").[[61]](#cite_note-61)

#### Modernising the party

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Harold_Macmillan.jpg/250px-Harold_Macmillan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harold_Macmillan.jpg)

[Harold Macmillan](/wiki/Harold_Macmillan "Harold Macmillan") is closely associated with the post-war settlement.

In 1947 the party published its [Industrial Charter](/wiki/Industrial_Charter "Industrial Charter") which marked its acceptance of the "[post-war consensus](/wiki/Post-war_consensus "Post-war consensus")" on the [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") and [labour rights](/wiki/Labour_rights "Labour rights").[[62]](#cite_note-62) [David Maxwell Fyfe](/wiki/David_Maxwell_Fyfe "David Maxwell Fyfe") chaired a committee into Conservative Party organisation that resulted in the Maxwell Fyfe Report (1948–49). The report required the party to do more fundraising, by forbidding constituency associations from demanding large donations from candidates, with the intention of broadening the [diversity](/wiki/Diversity_(politics) "Diversity (politics)") of MPs. In practice it may have had the effect of lending more power to [constituency](/wiki/United_Kingdom_constituencies "United Kingdom constituencies") parties and making candidates more uniform.[[63]](#cite_note-63) [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), the party leader, brought in a [party chairman](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Conservative_Party "Chairman of the Conservative Party") to modernise the party: [Frederick Marquis, 1st Earl of Woolton](/wiki/Frederick_Marquis,_1st_Earl_of_Woolton "Frederick Marquis, 1st Earl of Woolton"), rebuilt the local organisations with an emphasis on membership, money, and a unified national propaganda appeal on critical issues.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

With a narrow victory at the [1951 general election](/wiki/1951_United_Kingdom_general_election "1951 United Kingdom general election"), despite losing the popular vote, Churchill was back in power. Apart from rationing, which was ended in 1954, most of the [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state") enacted by Labour was accepted by the Conservatives and became part of the "post-war consensus" that was satirised as [Butskellism](/wiki/Butskellism "Butskellism") and that lasted until the 1970s.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) The Conservatives were conciliatory towards [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_unions_in_the_United_Kingdom "Trade unions in the United Kingdom"), but they did privatise the steel and road haulage industries in 1953.[[67]](#cite_note-67) During the Conservatives' thirteen-year tenure in office, pensions went up by 49% in real terms, sickness and unemployment benefits by 76% in real terms, and supplementary benefits by 46% in real terms. However, family allowances fell by 15% in real terms.[[68]](#cite_note-68) "Thirteen Wasted Years" was a popular slogan attacking the Conservative record, primarily from Labour. In addition, there were attacks by the right wing of the Conservative Party itself for its tolerance of socialist policies and reluctance to curb the legal powers of labour unions.

The Conservatives were re-elected [in 1955](/wiki/1955_United_Kingdom_general_election "1955 United Kingdom general election") and [in 1959](/wiki/1959_United_Kingdom_general_election "1959 United Kingdom general election") with larger majorities. The Conservative prime ministers Churchill, [Anthony Eden](/wiki/Anthony_Eden "Anthony Eden"), [Harold Macmillan](/wiki/Harold_Macmillan "Harold Macmillan") and [Alec Douglas-Home](/wiki/Alec_Douglas-Home "Alec Douglas-Home") promoted relatively liberal trade regulations and less state involvement throughout the 1950s and early 1960s. The [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis") of 1956 was a humiliating defeat for Eden, but his successor, Macmillan, minimised the damage and focused attention on domestic issues and prosperity. Following controversy over the selections of Macmillan and Douglas-Home via a process of consultation known as the 'Magic Circle',[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70) a formal election process was created and [the first leadership election](/wiki/1965_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "1965 Conservative Party leadership election") was held in 1965, won by [Edward Heath](/wiki/Edward_Heath "Edward Heath").[[71]](#cite_note-71)

#### 1965–1975: Edward Heath

[Heath's 1970–74 government](/wiki/Heath_ministry "Heath ministry") was known for taking the UK into the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community") (EEC), although the right wing of the party objected to his failure to control the trade unions at a time when a declining British industry saw many strikes, as well as the [1973–75 recession](/wiki/1973%E2%80%9375_recession "1973–75 recession"). Since accession to the EEC, which developed into the European Union, British membership has been a source of heated debate within the party.

Heath had come to power in [June 1970](/wiki/1970_United_Kingdom_general_election "1970 United Kingdom general election") and the last possible date for the next general election was not until mid-1975.[[72]](#cite_note-72) However, a general election was held in [February 1974](/wiki/February_1974_United_Kingdom_general_election "February 1974 United Kingdom general election") in a bid to win public support during [a national emergency](/wiki/Three-day_week "Three-day week") caused by the miners' strike. Heath's attempt to win a second term at this "snap" election failed, as a deadlock result [left no party with an overall majority](/wiki/Hung_parliament "Hung parliament"). Heath resigned within days, after failing to gain [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") support to form a coalition government. Labour won the [October 1974 election](/wiki/October_1974_United_Kingdom_general_election "October 1974 United Kingdom general election") with an overall majority of three seats.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

### 1975–1990: Margaret Thatcher

Further information: [Premiership of Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Premiership_of_Margaret_Thatcher "Premiership of Margaret Thatcher")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Margaret_Thatcher_%281983%29.jpg/250px-Margaret_Thatcher_%281983%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Margaret_Thatcher_(1983).jpg)

[Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") is the first woman to be Prime Minister and the [longest-serving](/wiki/List_of_prime_ministers_of_the_United_Kingdom_by_length_of_tenure "List of prime ministers of the United Kingdom by length of tenure") of the 20th century

Loss of power weakened Heath's control over the party and [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") deposed him at the [1975 leadership election](/wiki/1975_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "1975 Conservative Party leadership election"). Thatcher led her party to victory at the [1979 general election](/wiki/1979_United_Kingdom_general_election "1979 United Kingdom general election") with a manifesto which concentrated on the party's philosophy.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

As prime minister, Thatcher focused on rejecting the mild liberalism of the [post-war consensus](/wiki/Post-war_consensus "Post-war consensus") that tolerated or encouraged nationalisation, strong labour unions, heavy regulation, and high taxes.[[75]](#cite_note-75) She did not challenge the [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service"), and supported the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") policies of the consensus, but otherwise tried to dismantle and delegitimise it. She built a right-wing political ideology that became known as [Thatcherism](/wiki/Thatcherism "Thatcherism"), based on social and economic ideas from British and American intellectuals such as [Friedrich Hayek](/wiki/Friedrich_Hayek "Friedrich Hayek") and [Milton Friedman](/wiki/Milton_Friedman "Milton Friedman"). Thatcher believed that too much socially democratic-oriented government policy was leading to a long-term decline in the British economy. As a result, her government pursued a programme of [economic liberalism](/wiki/Economic_liberalism "Economic liberalism"), adopting a free-market approach to public services based on the sale of publicly owned industries and utilities, as well as a reduction in trade union power.

One of Thatcher's largest and most successful policies assisted council house tenants in public housing to purchase their homes at favourable rates. The "Right to Buy" had emerged in the late 1940s but was too great a challenge to the post-war consensus to win Conservative endorsement. Thatcher favoured the idea because it would lead to a "property-owning democracy", an important idea that had emerged in the 1920s.[[60]](#cite_note-Matthew_Francis_2011-60) Some local Conservative-run councils enacted profitable local sales schemes during the late 1960s. By the 1970s many working-class people could afford to buy homes, and eagerly adopted Thatcher's invitation to purchase their homes at a sizeable discount. The new owners were more likely to vote Conservative, as Thatcher had hoped.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

Thatcher led the Conservatives to two further electoral victories [in 1983](/wiki/1983_United_Kingdom_general_election "1983 United Kingdom general election") and [in 1987](/wiki/1987_United_Kingdom_general_election "1987 United Kingdom general election"). She was deeply unpopular in certain sections of society due to high unemployment and her response to the [miners' strike](/wiki/UK_miners%27_strike_(1984%E2%80%931985) "UK miners' strike (1984–1985)"). Unemployment had doubled between 1979 and 1982, largely due to Thatcher's [monetarist](/wiki/Monetarist "Monetarist") battle against inflation.[[78]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) At the time of the [1979 general election](/wiki/1979_United_Kingdom_general_election "1979 United Kingdom general election") inflation had been at 9% or under for the previous year, then increased to over 20% in the first two years of the Thatcher ministry, but it had fallen again to 5.8% by the start of 1983.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

The period of unpopularity of the Conservatives in the early 1980s coincided with a crisis in the Labour Party, which then formed the main opposition. Victory in the [Falklands War](/wiki/Falklands_War "Falklands War") in June 1982, along with the recovering British economy, saw the Conservatives returning quickly to the top of the opinion polls and winning the 1983 general election with a landslide majority, due to a split opposition vote.[[78]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-78) By the time of the general election in June 1987, the economy was stronger, with lower inflation and falling unemployment and Thatcher secured her third successive electoral victory.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

The introduction of the [Community Charge](/wiki/Community_Charge "Community Charge") (known by its opponents as the *poll tax*) in 1989 is often cited as contributing to her political downfall. Internal party tensions led to a leadership challenge by the Conservative MP [Michael Heseltine](/wiki/Michael_Heseltine "Michael Heseltine") and she resigned on 28 November 1990.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

### 1990–1997: John Major

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/John_Major_1996.jpg/250px-John_Major_1996.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Major_1996.jpg)

[John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major") succeeded Thatcher and led the Conservative government for seven years

[John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major") won the party leadership election on 27 November 1990, and his appointment led to an almost immediate boost in Conservative Party fortunes.[[83]](#cite_note-83) The [election](/wiki/1992_United_Kingdom_general_election "1992 United Kingdom general election") was held on 9 April 1992 and the Conservatives won a fourth successive electoral victory, contrary to predictions from opinion polls.[[84]](#cite_note-1992GE-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) The Conservatives became the first party to attract 14 million votes in a general election.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)

On 16 September 1992 the Government suspended Britain's membership of the [European Exchange Rate Mechanism](/wiki/European_Exchange_Rate_Mechanism "European Exchange Rate Mechanism") (ERM), after the pound fell lower than its minimum level in the ERM, a day thereafter referred to as [Black Wednesday](/wiki/Black_Wednesday "Black Wednesday").[[88]](#cite_note-88) Soon after, approximately one million householders faced repossession of their homes during a recession that saw a sharp rise in unemployment, taking it close to 3 million people.[[89]](#cite_note-:2-89) The party subsequently lost much of its reputation for good financial stewardship. The end of the recession was declared in April 1993.[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-:2-89) From 1994 to 1997 Major [privatised British Rail](/wiki/Privatisation_of_British_Rail "Privatisation of British Rail").

The party was plagued by internal division and infighting, mainly over the UK's role in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). The party's [Eurosceptic](/wiki/Euroscepticism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Euroscepticism in the United Kingdom") wing, represented by MPs such as [John Redwood](/wiki/John_Redwood "John Redwood"), opposed further EU integration, whilst the party's pro-European wing, represented by those such as Chancellor of the Exchequer [Kenneth Clarke](/wiki/Kenneth_Clarke "Kenneth Clarke"), was broadly supportive. The issue of the creation of a single European currency also inflamed tensions.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Major survived a leadership challenge in 1995 by Redwood, but Redwood received 89 votes, further undermining Major's influence.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

The Conservative government was increasingly accused in the media of "[sleaze](/wiki/Political_scandals_in_the_United_Kingdom "Political scandals in the United Kingdom")". Their support reached its lowest ebb in late 1994. Over the next two years the Conservatives gained some credit for the strong economic recovery and fall in unemployment.[[93]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-93) But an effective opposition campaign by the Labour Party culminated in a landslide defeat for the Conservatives [in 1997](/wiki/1997_United_Kingdom_general_election "1997 United Kingdom general election"), their worst defeat since the [1906 general election](/wiki/1906_United_Kingdom_general_election "1906 United Kingdom general election"). The 1997 election left the Conservative Party as an England-only party, with all Scottish and Welsh seats having been lost, and not a single new seat having been gained anywhere.

### 1997–2010: Political wilderness

Major resigned as party leader and was succeeded by [William Hague](/wiki/William_Hague "William Hague").[[94]](#cite_note-1997OTD-94) The [2001 general election](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_general_election "2001 United Kingdom general election") resulted in a net gain of one seat for the Conservative Party and returned a mostly unscathed Labour Party back to government.[[95]](#cite_note-95) This all occurred months after the [fuel protests of September 2000](/wiki/Fuel_protests_in_the_United_Kingdom "Fuel protests in the United Kingdom") had seen the Conservatives briefly take a narrow lead over Labour in the opinion polls.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

In 2001 [Iain Duncan Smith](/wiki/Iain_Duncan_Smith "Iain Duncan Smith") was [elected leader of the party](/wiki/2001_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "2001 Conservative Party leadership election").[[94]](#cite_note-1997OTD-94) Although Duncan Smith was a strong [Eurosceptic](/wiki/Eurosceptic "Eurosceptic"),[[97]](#cite_note-97) during his tenure, Europe ceased to be an issue of division in the party as it united behind calls for a referendum on the proposed [European Union Constitution](/wiki/European_Union_Constitution "European Union Constitution").[[98]](#cite_note-98) However, before he could lead the party into a general election, Duncan Smith lost the vote on a [motion of no confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence") by MPs.[[99]](#cite_note-99) This was despite the Conservative support equalling that of Labour in the months leading up to his departure from the leadership.[[93]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-93)

[Michael Howard](/wiki/Michael_Howard "Michael Howard") then [stood for the leadership unopposed](/wiki/2003_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "2003 Conservative Party leadership election") on 6 November 2003.[[100]](#cite_note-100) Under Howard's leadership at the [2005 general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election"), the Conservative Party increased their total vote share and—more significantly—their number of parliamentary seats, reducing Labour's majority.[[101]](#cite_note-101) The day following the election, Howard resigned.

[David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") won the [2005 leadership election](/wiki/2005_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "2005 Conservative Party leadership election").[[102]](#cite_note-102) He then announced his intention to reform and realign the Conservatives.[[103]](#cite_note-Cameron-speech-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104) For most of 2006 and the first half of 2007, polls showed leads over Labour for the Conservatives.[[105]](#cite_note-105) Polls became more volatile in summer 2007 with the accession of [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") as prime minister. The Conservatives gained control of the [London mayoralty](/wiki/Mayor_of_London "Mayor of London") for the first time in 2008 after [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") defeated the Labour incumbent, [Ken Livingstone](/wiki/Ken_Livingstone "Ken Livingstone").[[106]](#cite_note-106)

### 2010–2024: Austerity, Brexit, and the pandemic

The Conservative Party came to government in May 2010, first under [a coalition](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition") with the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") and later as a series of majority and minority governments. During this period there were five Conservative Prime Ministers: Cameron, [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May"), Johnson, [Liz Truss](/wiki/Liz_Truss "Liz Truss") and [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak").

The initial period of this time, primarily during [Cameron's premiership](/wiki/Premiership_of_David_Cameron "Premiership of David Cameron"), was marked by the ongoing effects of the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") and the implementation of [austerity measures](/wiki/United_Kingdom_government_austerity_programme "United Kingdom government austerity programme") in response. From 2015 the predominant political event was the [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") referendum and the process to implement the decision to leave the trade bloc. Subsequent to the EU referendum vote, and through the premierships of May, Johnson and their successors, the party shifted right on the political spectrum.[[21]](#cite_note-accu-21)

The Conservatives' time in office was marked by several controversies. The presence of [Islamophobia in the Conservative Party](/wiki/Islamophobia_in_the_UK_Conservative_Party "Islamophobia in the UK Conservative Party"), including allegations against its policies, fringes, and structure, was often in the public eye. These include allegations against senior politicians such as Johnson, [Michael Gove](/wiki/Michael_Gove "Michael Gove"), May and [Zac Goldsmith](/wiki/Zac_Goldsmith "Zac Goldsmith"). During the period of the Cameron[[107]](#cite_note-Cowburn_2017_x541-107)[[108]](#cite_note-Cohen_2017_r220-108)[[109]](#cite_note-Watts_2017_y333-109) and Johnson governments,[[110]](#cite_note-Halliday_2022_r197-110) a number of Conservative MPs were accused or convicted of sexual misconduct, with cases including the consumption of pornography in parliament, rape, groping and sexual harassment.[[111]](#cite_note-Savage_Helm_2017_o437-111)[[112]](#cite_note-ITV_News_2022_f647-112)[[113]](#cite_note-Samuelson_2022_z079-113) In 2017 a list of 36 sitting Conservative MPs accused of inappropriate sexual behaviour was leaked. The list is believed to have been compiled by party staff.[[114]](#cite_note-Simpson_2017_f842-114) Following accusations of multiple cases of rape against an unnamed Conservative MP in 2023[[115]](#cite_note-Blewitt_2023_c778-115) and allegations of a cover-up,[[116]](#cite_note-BBC_News_2023_s403-116)[[117]](#cite_note-Crerar_2023_t116-117) [Baroness Warsi](/wiki/Sayeeda_Warsi,_Baroness_Warsi "Sayeeda Warsi, Baroness Warsi"), who has served as the party's co-chairman under Cameron, stated that the Conservative Party has had a problem handling complaints of sexual misconducts against members appropriately.[[118]](#cite_note-Wright_2023_i317-118)

#### 2010–2016: David Cameron

Main article: [Premiership of David Cameron](/wiki/Premiership_of_David_Cameron "Premiership of David Cameron")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Prime_Minister_David_Cameron_-_official_photograph_%288947770804%29.jpg/250px-Prime_Minister_David_Cameron_-_official_photograph_%288947770804%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_Minister_David_Cameron_-_official_photograph_(8947770804).jpg)

[David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") implemented [austerity programme](/wiki/United_Kingdom_government_austerity_programme "United Kingdom government austerity programme") after the financial crisis

The election in May 2010 resulted in a [hung parliament](/wiki/Hung_parliament "Hung parliament") with the Conservatives having the most seats but short of an overall majority.[[119]](#cite_note-2010GEresults-119) Following the resignation of [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown"), Cameron was named prime minister, and the Conservatives [entered government in a coalition](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition") with the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)")—the first postwar [coalition government](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government").[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121) Cameron's premiership was marked by the effects of the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"); these involved a large deficit in government finances that his government sought to reduce through controversial [austerity measures](/wiki/United_Kingdom_government_austerity_programme "United Kingdom government austerity programme").[[122]](#cite_note-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123) In September 2014 the Unionist side, championed by Labour as well as by the Conservative Party and the Liberal Democrats, won in the [Scottish Independence referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum") by 55% No to 45% Yes on the question "Should Scotland be an independent country?"[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-125)

At the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election") the Conservatives formed a majority government under Cameron.[[126]](#cite_note-126) After speculation of a referendum on the UK's EU membership throughout his premiership, a vote was announced for June 2016 in which Cameron campaigned to remain in the EU.[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128) On 24 June 2016 Cameron announced his intention to resign as prime minister after he failed to convince the British public to [stay in the European Union](/wiki/EU_Referendum_UK "EU Referendum UK").[[129]](#cite_note-129)

#### 2016–2022: Theresa May and Boris Johnson

Main articles: [Premiership of Theresa May](/wiki/Premiership_of_Theresa_May "Premiership of Theresa May") and [Premiership of Boris Johnson](/wiki/Premiership_of_Boris_Johnson "Premiership of Boris Johnson")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Theresa_May_%282016%29_02.jpg/250px-Theresa_May_%282016%29_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Theresa_May_(2016)_02.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Boris_Johnson_official_portrait.jpg/250px-Boris_Johnson_official_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boris_Johnson_official_portrait.jpg)

[Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") oversaw Brexit negotiations, which would be finalised by her successor [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson"), before leading the country to combat the [pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom")

On 11 July 2016 [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") became the leader of the Conservative Party.[[130]](#cite_note-130) May promised social reform and a more centrist political outlook for the Conservative Party and its government.[[131]](#cite_note-131) May's early cabinet appointments were interpreted as an effort to reunite the party in the wake of the UK's [vote to leave the European Union](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum").[[132]](#cite_note-132) She began the process of [withdrawing the UK from the European Union](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") in March 2017.[[133]](#cite_note-133) In April 2017 the Cabinet agreed to hold [a general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election") on 8 June.[[134]](#cite_note-134) In a shock result, the election resulted in a [hung parliament](/wiki/Hung_parliament "Hung parliament"), with the Conservative Party needing a [confidence and supply](/wiki/Conservative%E2%80%93DUP_agreement "Conservative–DUP agreement") arrangement with the [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") to support a minority government.[[135]](#cite_note-135)[[136]](#cite_note-136)

May's premiership was dominated by Brexit as she carried out negotiations with the European Union, adhering to the [Chequers Plan](/wiki/Chequers_Plan "Chequers Plan"), which resulted in her draft [Brexit withdrawal agreement](/wiki/Brexit_withdrawal_agreement "Brexit withdrawal agreement").[[137]](#cite_note-137) May survived two votes of no confidence in December 2018 and January 2019, but after versions of her draft withdrawal agreement were [rejected by Parliament three times](/wiki/Parliamentary_votes_on_Brexit "Parliamentary votes on Brexit"), May announced her resignation on 24 May 2019.[[138]](#cite_note-138) In July 2019 [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") became the leader of the party and the prime minister.[[139]](#cite_note-bbc230719-139)

Johnson had made withdrawal from the EU by 31 October "with no ifs, buts or maybes" a key pledge during [his campaign for the party leadership](/wiki/2019_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "2019 Conservative Party leadership election").[[140]](#cite_note-140) He lost his working majority in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") on 3 September 2019.[[141]](#cite_note-bbc030919-141) Later that same day, [21 Conservative MPs had the Conservative whip withdrawn](/wiki/September_2019_suspension_of_rebel_Conservative_MPs "September 2019 suspension of rebel Conservative MPs") after voting with the Opposition to grant the Commons control over its order paper.[[142]](#cite_note-142) Johnson would later halt the [Withdrawal Agreement Bill](/wiki/Withdrawal_Agreement_Bill "Withdrawal Agreement Bill"), calling for a general election.[[143]](#cite_note-143) The [2019 general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election") resulted in the Conservatives winning a majority, the party's largest since [1987](/wiki/1987_United_Kingdom_general_election "1987 United Kingdom general election").[[144]](#cite_note-bbc131219-144) It won several constituencies, particularly in [formerly traditional Labour seats](/wiki/Red_Wall_(British_politics) "Red Wall (British politics)").[[30]](#cite_note-The_Independent-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Mueller-31) On 20 December 2019, MPs passed an agreement for withdrawing from the EU; the United Kingdom formally left on 31 January 2020.[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146)

Johnson presided over the UK's response to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom").[[147]](#cite_note-The_Guardian-147) From late 2021 onwards, Johnson received huge public backlash for the [Partygate](/wiki/Partygate "Partygate") scandal, in which staff and senior members of government were pictured holding gatherings during lockdown contrary to Government guidance.[[148]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-148) The [Metropolitan Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police "Metropolitan Police") eventually fined Johnson for breaking lockdown rules in April 2022.[[149]](#cite_note-149) In July 2022 Johnson admitted to having appointed [Chris Pincher](/wiki/Chris_Pincher "Chris Pincher") as deputy chief whip while being aware of allegations of sexual assault against him.[[150]](#cite_note-150) This, along with Partygate and increasing criticisms on Johnson's handling of the cost-of-living crisis, provoked [a government crisis](/wiki/July_2022_United_Kingdom_government_crisis "July 2022 United Kingdom government crisis") following a loss in confidence and nearly 60 resignations from government officials, eventually leading to Johnson announcing his resignation on 7 July.[[151]](#cite_note-151)[[152]](#cite_note-152)

#### 2022–24: Liz Truss and Rishi Sunak

Main articles: [Premiership of Liz Truss](/wiki/Premiership_of_Liz_Truss "Premiership of Liz Truss") and [Premiership of Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Premiership_of_Rishi_Sunak "Premiership of Rishi Sunak")

Johnson's successor as leader was confirmed as [Liz Truss](/wiki/Liz_Truss "Liz Truss") on 5 September, following [a leadership election](/wiki/July%E2%80%93September_2022_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "July–September 2022 Conservative Party leadership election").[[153]](#cite_note-153) In a strategy labelled [Trussonomics](/wiki/Trussonomics "Trussonomics") she introduced policies in response to the [cost-of-living crisis](/wiki/2021%E2%80%932022_United_Kingdom_cost_of_living_crisis "2021–2022 United Kingdom cost of living crisis"),[[154]](#cite_note-154) including price caps on energy bills and government help to pay them.[[155]](#cite_note-155) [Truss's mini-budget](/wiki/Truss%27s_mini-budget "Truss's mini-budget") on 23 September faced severe criticism and markets reacted poorly;[[156]](#cite_note-156) the pound fell to a record low of 1.03 against the US dollar, and [UK government gilt](/wiki/Gilt-edged_securities "Gilt-edged securities") yields rose to 4.3 per cent, prompting the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") to trigger an emergency bond-buying programme.[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158) After condemnation from the public, the Labour Party and her own party, Truss reversed some aspects of the mini-budget, including the abolition of the top rate of income tax.[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160) Following [a government crisis](/wiki/October_2022_United_Kingdom_government_crisis "October 2022 United Kingdom government crisis") Truss announced her resignation as prime minister on 20 October[[161]](#cite_note-BBC_News-161) after 44 days in office, the shortest premiership in British history.[[161]](#cite_note-BBC_News-161)[[162]](#cite_note-162) Truss also oversaw the worst polling the Conservatives had ever received, with Labour polling as high as 36 per cent above the Conservatives amidst the crisis.[[163]](#cite_note-163)

On 24 October 2022 [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak") was declared leader, the first [British Asian](/wiki/British_Asian "British Asian") leader of the Conservatives and the first British Asian prime minister. On 22 May 2024 Sunak announced a general election to be held on 4 July 2024.[[164]](#cite_note-164) During the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election"), public opinion in favour of a change in government was reflected by poor polling from the Conservative Party, with [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK") making strong polling gains.[[165]](#cite_note-165) The Conservative manifesto focused on the economy, taxes, welfare, expanding free childcare, education, healthcare, environment, energy, transport and crime.[[166]](#cite_note-166)[[167]](#cite_note-167) It pledged to lower taxes, increase education and NHS spending, deliver 92,000 more nurses and 28,000 more doctors, introduce a new model of [National Service](/wiki/National_service "National service"), and to treble Britain's offshore wind capacity and support solar energy.[[168]](#cite_note-168)[[169]](#cite_note-169) The final result was the lowest seat total at a general election in the history of the Conservative Party, with well below the previous record low of 156 seats won at the [1906 general election](/wiki/1906_United_Kingdom_general_election "1906 United Kingdom general election").[[170]](#cite_note-170) Sunak resigned as Prime Minister and the Conservative Party is ousted from the government.

### 2024–: In opposition

[Kemi Badenoch](/wiki/Kemi_Badenoch "Kemi Badenoch") became party leader, leading the Conservatives in opposition.[[171]](#cite_note-z459-171) As Reform UK surged and topped the opinion polls, the Conservatives faced a [number of defections](/wiki/List_of_Conservative_Party_defections_to_Reform_UK "List of Conservative Party defections to Reform UK") of MPs, including former Home Secretary [Suella Braverman](/wiki/Suella_Braverman "Suella Braverman") and shadow Justice Secretary [Robert Jenrick](/wiki/Robert_Jenrick "Robert Jenrick").
