The Conservative Party believes that a [free market](/wiki/Free_market "Free market") and individual achievement are the primary factors behind economic prosperity. A leading economic theory advocated by Conservatives is [supply-side economics](/wiki/Supply-side_economics "Supply-side economics"), which holds that reduced income tax rates increase growth and enterprise (although a reduction in the budget deficit has sometimes taken priority over cutting taxes).[[172]](#cite_note-172) The party focuses on the [social market economy](/wiki/Social_market_economy "Social market economy"), promoting a free market for competition with social balance to create fairness. This has included education reform, vocational skills reform, expanding free childcare, curbs on the banking sector, enterprise zones to revive regions in Britain, and grand and extensive infrastructure projects, such as high-speed rail.[[173]](#cite_note-173)[[174]](#cite_note-174)

One concrete economic policy of recent years has been opposition to the European single currency, the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro"). With the growing [Euroscepticism](/wiki/Euroscepticism "Euroscepticism") within his party, [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major") negotiated a British opt-out in the 1992 [Maastricht Treaty](/wiki/Maastricht_Treaty "Maastricht Treaty"), which enabled the UK to stay within the European Union without adopting the single currency. All subsequent Conservative leaders have positioned the party firmly against the adoption of the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro").

The 50% top rate of [income tax](/wiki/UK_income_tax "UK income tax") was reduced to 45% by the Conservative–Liberal Democrat coalition.[[175]](#cite_note-175) Alongside a reduction in tax and commitments to keep taxation low, the Conservative Party has significantly reduced government spending, through the [austerity programme](/wiki/United_Kingdom_government_austerity_programme "United Kingdom government austerity programme") which commenced in 2010, subsequent to the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"). In 2019 and during the election campaign that year, Boris Johnson signalled an end to austerity with increased public expenditure, in areas including healthcare, education, transport, welfare, and the police.[[176]](#cite_note-176)[[177]](#cite_note-177)

#### Jobs and welfare policy

One of the Conservatives' key policy goals in 2010 was to reduce the number of people unemployed, and increase the number of people in the workforce, by strengthening apprenticeships, skills and job training.[[178]](#cite_note-178) Between 2010 and 2014, all claimants of [Incapacity Benefit](/wiki/Incapacity_Benefit "Incapacity Benefit") were moved onto a new benefit scheme, [Employment and Support Allowance](/wiki/Employment_and_Support_Allowance "Employment and Support Allowance"), which was then subsumed into the [Universal Credit](/wiki/Universal_Credit "Universal Credit") system alongside other welfare benefits in 2018.[[179]](#cite_note-179)[[180]](#cite_note-180)[[181]](#cite_note-181) The Universal Credit system came under immense scrutiny following its introduction. Shortly after her appointment to the [Department for Work and Pensions](/wiki/Department_for_Work_and_Pensions "Department for Work and Pensions"), the then Secretary of State [Amber Rudd](/wiki/Amber_Rudd "Amber Rudd") acknowledged there were real problems with the Universal Credit system, especially the wait times for initial payments and the housing payments aspect of the combined benefits.[[182]](#cite_note-RiNov18-182) Rudd pledged specifically to review and address the uneven impact of Universal Credit implementation on economically disadvantaged women, which had been the subject of numerous reports by the Radio 4 *[You and Yours](/wiki/You_and_Yours "You and Yours")* programme and others.[[182]](#cite_note-RiNov18-182)

Until 1999 Conservatives opposed the creation of a [national minimum wage](/wiki/National_minimum_wage "National minimum wage"), as they believed it would cost jobs, and businesses would be reluctant to start business in the UK from fear of high labour costs.[[183]](#cite_note-183) However the party have since pledged support and in the [July 2015 budget](/wiki/July_2015_United_Kingdom_budget "July 2015 United Kingdom budget"), Chancellor George Osborne announced a [National Living Wage](/wiki/National_Living_Wage "National Living Wage") of £9/hour.[[184]](#cite_note-measures-184) The National Minimum Wage in 2024 was £11.44 for those over 21.[[185]](#cite_note-185) The party support, and have implemented, the restoration of the link between pensions and earnings, and seek to raise retirement age from 65 to 67 by 2028.[[186]](#cite_note-186)

#### Transport and infrastructure policy

The Conservatives have invested in public transport and infrastructure, aimed to promoting economic growth.[[187]](#cite_note-187) This has included rail (including [high-speed rail](/wiki/High-speed_rail "High-speed rail")), electric vehicles, bus networks, and [active transport](/wiki/Sustainable_transport "Sustainable transport").[[188]](#cite_note-188)

In 2020 new funding for active travel infrastructure was announced by the Conservatives.[[189]](#cite_note-189) The party's stated aim was for England to be a "great walking and cycling nation" and for half of all journeys in towns and cities being walked or cycled by 2030. The plan was accompanied by £2 billion in additional funding over the following five years for cycling and walking. The plan also introduced new inspectorate, known as [Active Travel England](/wiki/Active_Travel_England "Active Travel England").[[190]](#cite_note-190)[[191]](#cite_note-191)

In 2021 the Conservatives announced a white paper that would transform the operation of the railways. The rail network will be partly renationalised, with infrastructure and operations brought together under the state-owned public body [Great British Railways](/wiki/Great_British_Railways "Great British Railways").[[192]](#cite_note-BBC_News2-192) On 18 November 2021, the government announced the biggest ever public investment in Britain's rail network costing £96 billion and promising quicker and more frequent rail connections in the North and Midlands: the [Integrated Rail Plan](/wiki/Integrated_Rail_Plan_for_the_North_and_Midlands "Integrated Rail Plan for the North and Midlands") includes substantially improved connections north–south as well as east–west and includes three new high speed lines.[[193]](#cite_note-Gov-193)[[194]](#cite_note-194)

#### Energy and climate change policy

[David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") brought several '[green](/wiki/Green_conservatism "Green conservatism")' issues to the forefront of his 2010 campaign. These included proposals designed to impose a tax on workplace car parking spaces, a halt to airport growth, a tax on cars with exceptionally poor petrol mileage, and restrictions on car advertising. Many of these policies were implemented in the Coalition—including the '[Green Deal](/wiki/The_Green_Deal "The Green Deal")'.[[195]](#cite_note-195) A law was passed in 2019 that [UK greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") will be [net zero](/wiki/Net_zero "Net zero") by 2050.[[196]](#cite_note-:3-196) The UK was the first major economy to embrace a legal obligation to achieve net zero carbon emissions.[[197]](#cite_note-197)

In 2019 the Conservatives became the first national government in the world to officially declare a climate emergency (second in the UK after the [SNP](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party")).[[196]](#cite_note-:3-196) In November 2020 the Conservatives announced a 10-point plan for a 'green industrial revolution', with [green enterprises](/wiki/Sustainable_business "Sustainable business"), an end to the sale of petrol and diesel cars, quadruple the amount of [offshore wind power](/wiki/Wind_power_in_the_United_Kingdom "Wind power in the United Kingdom") capacity within a decade, fund a variety of emissions-cutting proposals, and spurn a proposed [green post-COVID-19 recovery](/wiki/Green_recovery "Green recovery").[[198]](#cite_note-198) In 2021, the Conservatives announced plans to cut [carbon emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions_by_the_United_Kingdom "Greenhouse gas emissions by the United Kingdom") by 78% by 2035.[[199]](#cite_note-199)

### Social policy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Conservative_and_Proud_%284764127652%29.jpg/250px-Conservative_and_Proud_%284764127652%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Conservative_and_Proud_(4764127652).jpg)

Conservative Party supporters at Pride London in 2010

Socially conservative policies such as tax incentives for married couples may have played a role in the party's electoral decline in the 1990s and early 2000s, and so the party has attempted to seek a new direction. As part of their coalition with the Liberal Democrats, the Conservative government did support the introduction of equal marriage rights for LGBT+ individuals in 2010, though 139 Conservative MPs, a majority, voted against [the 2013 same-sex marriage act](/wiki/Marriage_(Same_Sex_Couples)_Act_2013 "Marriage (Same Sex Couples) Act 2013"). Thus the extent to which this policy represented a more liberal Conservative party has been challenged.[[206]](#cite_note-marequa-206)

Since 1997 debate has occurred within the party between 'modernisers' such as [Alan Duncan](/wiki/Alan_Duncan "Alan Duncan"),[[207]](#cite_note-207) who believe that the Conservatives should modify their public stances on social issues, and 'traditionalists' such as [Liam Fox](/wiki/Liam_Fox "Liam Fox")[[208]](#cite_note-208)[[209]](#cite_note-209) and [Owen Paterson](/wiki/Owen_Paterson "Owen Paterson"),[[210]](#cite_note-210) who believe that the party should remain faithful to its traditional conservative platform. In the previous parliament, modernising forces were represented by MPs such as [Neil O'Brien](/wiki/Neil_O%27Brien "Neil O'Brien"), who has argued that the party needs to renew its policies and image, and is said to be inspired by [Macron](/wiki/Emmanuel_Macron "Emmanuel Macron")'s centrist politics.[[211]](#cite_note-211) [Ruth Davidson](/wiki/Ruth_Davidson "Ruth Davidson") is also seen as a reforming figure. Many of the original 'traditionalists' remain influential, though Duncan Smith's influence in terms of Commons contributions has waned.[[212]](#cite_note-212)

The party has strongly criticised what it describes as Labour's "state [multiculturalism](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism")".[[213]](#cite_note-213) Shadow Home Secretary Dominic Grieve said in 2008 that state multiculturalism policies had created a "terrible" legacy of "cultural despair" and dislocation, which has encouraged support for "extremists" on both sides of the debate.[[214]](#cite_note-:0-214) David Cameron responded to Grieve's comments by agreeing that policies of "state multiculturalism" that treat social groups as distinct, for example policies that "treat British Muslims as Muslims, rather than as British citizens", are wrong. However, he expressed support for the premise of multiculturalism on the whole.[[214]](#cite_note-:0-214)

Official statistics showed that EU and non-EU [mass immigration](/wiki/Mass_immigration "Mass immigration"), together with [asylum seeker](/wiki/Asylum_seeker "Asylum seeker") applications, all increased substantially during Cameron's term in office.[[215]](#cite_note-reuters-215)[[216]](#cite_note-reuters2-216)[[217]](#cite_note-independent-217) However, this was not solely as a result of intentional government policy – during this period, there were significant refugee flows into the UK and an increased level of asylum applications due to conflict and persecution globally.[[218]](#cite_note-218)[[219]](#cite_note-219) In 2019 former Conservative Home Secretary [Priti Patel](/wiki/Priti_Patel "Priti Patel") announced that the government would enact stricter immigration reforms, crack down on illegal immigration, and scrap freedom of movement with the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") following the completion of [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit").[[220]](#cite_note-220) In the four years following this announcement, net migration increased annually, in large part due to the number of health care workers, and their dependents, that were invited into the country because of recruitment problems caused by Brexit and the pandemic.[[221]](#cite_note-Taylor_2021_y110-221)[[222]](#cite_note-Atkins_2024_r712-222) The number of asylum seekers dropped as a proportion of total net migrants, whereas the number of people coming to the UK to study increased during that time period.[[223]](#cite_note-Financial_Times_d753-223)

#### Family policy

As prime minister, [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") wanted to 'support family life in Britain' and put families at the centre of domestic social policymaking.[[224]](#cite_note-:1-224) He stated in 2014 that there was 'no better place to start' in the Conservative mission of 'building society from the bottom up' than the family, which was responsible for individual welfare and well-being long before the welfare state came into play.[[224]](#cite_note-:1-224) He also argued that 'family and politics are inextricably linked'.[[224]](#cite_note-:1-224) Both Cameron and [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") aimed at helping families achieve a work-home balance and have previously proposed to offer all parents 12 months parental leave, to be shared by parents as they choose.[[225]](#cite_note-225) This policy is now in place, offering 50 weeks total parental leave, of which 37 weeks are paid leave, which can be shared between both parents.[[226]](#cite_note-226)

Other policies have included doubling the free hours of childcare for working parents of three and four-year-olds from 15 hours to 30 hours a week during term-time, although parents can reduce the number of hours per week to 22 and spread across 52 weeks of the year. The government also introduced a policy to fund 15 hours a week of free education and childcare for 2-year-olds in England if parents are receiving certain state benefits or the child has a SEN statement or diagnosis, worth £2,500 a year per child.[[227]](#cite_note-227)[[228]](#cite_note-228)

#### Health and drug policy

In 1945 the Conservatives declared support for universal healthcare.[[229]](#cite_note-229) They introduced the [Health and Social Care Act 2012](/wiki/Health_and_Social_Care_Act_2012 "Health and Social Care Act 2012"), constituting the biggest reformation that the NHS has ever undertaken.[[230]](#cite_note-230)

The Conservative Party supports [drug prohibition](/wiki/Drug_prohibition "Drug prohibition").[[231]](#cite_note-231) Legalisation of [cannabis for some medical uses](/wiki/Medical_cannabis "Medical cannabis") is favoured by some Conservative politicians.[[232]](#cite_note-232) The party has rejected both decriminalising drugs for personal use and the creation of [safe consumption sites](/wiki/Supervised_injection_site "Supervised injection site").[[233]](#cite_note-233) In 2024 the Conservative government passed a law to phase out smoking by banning the sale of tobacco to anyone born in or after 2009.[[234]](#cite_note-234)

### Foreign policy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Reagan%27s_-_Thatcher%27s_c50515-16.jpg/250px-Reagan%27s_-_Thatcher%27s_c50515-16.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reagan%27s_-_Thatcher%27s_c50515-16.jpg)

Margaret Thatcher (second left), [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") (far left) and their spouses in 1988. Thatcher and Reagan developed a close relationship against the Soviet Union.

For much of the 20th century, the Conservative Party took a broadly [Atlanticist](/wiki/Atlanticist "Atlanticist") stance in relations with the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), Members of [EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), favouring close ties with the United States and similarly aligned nations such as [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), [Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"). The Conservatives have generally favoured a diverse range of international alliances, ranging from the [North Atlantic Treaty Organization](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Treaty_Organization "North Atlantic Treaty Organization") (NATO) to the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"). The Conservatives have proposed a Pan-African [Free Trade Area](/wiki/Free_Trade_Area "Free Trade Area"), which it says could help [entrepreneurial](/wiki/Entrepreneurial "Entrepreneurial") dynamism of African people.[[235]](#cite_note-cameronbbc-235) The Conservatives pledged to increase aid spending to 0.7% of national income by 2013.[[235]](#cite_note-cameronbbc-235) They met this pledge in 2014, when spending on aid reached 0.72% of GDP and the commitment was enshrined in UK law in 2015.[[236]](#cite_note-236)

Close [Anglo-American Relationship](/wiki/United_Kingdom-United_States_relations "United Kingdom-United States relations") have been an element of Conservative foreign policy since the Second World War. Though the Anglo–American relationship in foreign affairs has often been termed a '[Special Relationship](/wiki/Special_Relationship "Special Relationship")', a term coined by [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), this has often been observed most clearly where leaders in each country are of a similar political stripe. [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") had sought to distance himself from former [US President](/wiki/President_of_United_States "President of United States") [Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") and his [neoconservative](/wiki/Neoconservative "Neoconservative") foreign policy.[[237]](#cite_note-237) Despite traditional links between the UK Conservatives and US [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), London Mayor [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson"), a Conservative, endorsed [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") in the 2008 election.[[238]](#cite_note-238) However, after becoming prime minister, Johnson developed a close relationship with Republican President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump").[[239]](#cite_note-239)[[240]](#cite_note-240)[[241]](#cite_note-241) This has been described as a reestablishing of the *Special Relationship* with the United States following Britain's withdraw from the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), as well as returning to the links between the Conservatives and Republican Party.[[242]](#cite_note-242) Beyond relations with the United States, the Commonwealth and the EU, the Conservative Party has generally supported a pro free-trade foreign policy within the mainstream of international affairs.

Although stances have changed with successive leadership, the modern Conservative Party generally supports cooperation and maintaining friendly relations with [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"). Historic Conservative statesmen such as [Arthur Balfour](/wiki/Arthur_Balfour "Arthur Balfour") and [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") supported the idea of national home for the Jewish people. Under [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") Conservative support for Israel was seen to crystallise.[[243]](#cite_note-243)[[244]](#cite_note-244) Support for Israel increased under the leaderships of [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") and [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson"), with prominent Conservative figures within the May and Johnson ministries strongly endorsing Israel. In 2016, Theresa May publicly rebutted[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] statements made by US Secretary of State [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") over the composition of the Israeli government.[[245]](#cite_note-245)[[246]](#cite_note-246) In 2018 the party pledged to proscribe all wings of the Lebanese-based militant group [Hezbollah](/wiki/Hezbollah "Hezbollah") and this was adopted as a UK-wide policy in 2019.[[247]](#cite_note-247)[[248]](#cite_note-248) In 2019, the Conservative government under Boris Johnson announced plans to stop the influence of the [Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions](/wiki/Boycott,_Divestment_and_Sanctions "Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions") movement on local politics which included prohibiting local councils from boycotting Israeli products.[[249]](#cite_note-249)[[250]](#cite_note-250)[[251]](#cite_note-251)

#### Defence policy

After the [terrorist attacks of 11 September 2001](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"), the Conservative Party supported the coalition military action in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"). The Conservative Party believed that success in Afghanistan would be defined in terms of the Afghans achieving the capability to maintain their own internal and external security. They have repeatedly criticised the former [Labour Government](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") for failing to equip British Forces adequately in the earlier days on the campaign—especially highlighting the shortage of helicopters for British Forces resulting from [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown")'s £1.4bn cut to the helicopter budget in 2004.[[252]](#cite_note-252)

The Conservative Party believes that in the 21st century defence and security are interlinked. It has pledged to break away from holding a traditional [Strategic Defence Review](/wiki/Defence_Review "Defence Review") and committed to carrying out a more comprehensive [Strategic Defence and Security Review](/wiki/Strategic_Defence_and_Security_Review_2010 "Strategic Defence and Security Review 2010") (SDSR).[[253]](#cite_note-253) As well as an SDSR, the Conservative Party pledged in 2010 to undertake a fundamental and far-reaching review of the procurement process and how defence equipment is provided in Britain, and to increase Britain's share of the global defence market as Government policy.[[254]](#cite_note-254)

The Conservative Party upholds the view that [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") remains and should remain the most important security alliance for the United Kingdom.[[255]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-255) It has advocated for the creation of a fairer funding mechanism for NATO's expeditionary operations and called for all NATO countries to meet their required defence spending 2% of GDP. Some Conservatives believe that there is scope for expanding NATO's Article V to include new threats such as [cybersecurity](/wiki/Cybersecurity "Cybersecurity").

The Conservative Party aims to build enhanced bilateral defence relations with key European partners and believes that it is in Britain's national interest to cooperate fully with all its European neighbours. It has pledged to ensure that any EU military capability must supplement and not supplant British national defence and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), and that it is not in the British interest to hand over security to any supranational body.[[255]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-255)

The Conservatives see it as a priority to encourage all members of the European Union to do more in terms of a commitment to European security at home and abroad. Regarding the defence role of the European Union, the Conservatives pledged to re-examine some of Britain's EU Defence commitments to determine their practicality and utility; specifically, to reassess UK participation provisions like Permanent Structured Cooperation, the [European Defence Agency](/wiki/European_Defence_Agency "European Defence Agency") and EU Battlegroups to determine if there is any value in Britain's participation.

The Conservatives support the UK's possession of nuclear weapons through the [Trident nuclear programme](/wiki/Trident_(UK_nuclear_programme) "Trident (UK nuclear programme)").[[255]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-255)

#### European Union policy

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(February 2026)* |

No subject has proved more divisive in the Conservative Party in recent history than the role of the United Kingdom within the European Union. Though the principal architect of the UK's entry into the [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities") (which became the European Union) was Conservative Prime Minister [Edward Heath](/wiki/Edward_Heath "Edward Heath"), most contemporary Conservative opinion is opposed to closer economic and particularly political union with the EU. This is a noticeable shift in British politics, as in the 1960s and 1970s the Conservatives were more pro-Europe than the Labour Party: for example, in the 1971 House of Commons vote on whether the UK should join the European Economic Community, only 39 of the then 330 Conservative MPs were opposed.[[256]](#cite_note-georgiou-256)[[257]](#cite_note-257)

The Conservative Party has members with varying opinions of the EU, with [pro-European](/wiki/Pro-European "Pro-European") Conservatives joining the affiliate [Conservative Group for Europe](/wiki/Conservative_Group_for_Europe "Conservative Group for Europe"), while some Eurosceptics left the party to join the [United Kingdom Independence Party](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Independence_Party "United Kingdom Independence Party"). Whilst the vast majority of Conservatives in recent decades have been Eurosceptics, views among this group regarding the UK's relationship with the EU have been polarised between moderate, soft Eurosceptics who support continued British membership but oppose further harmonisation of regulations affecting business and accept participation in a [multi-speed Europe](/wiki/Multi-speed_Europe "Multi-speed Europe"), and a more radical, economically libertarian faction who oppose policy initiatives from Brussels, support the rolling back of integration measures from the Maastricht Treaty onwards, and have become increasingly supportive of a complete withdrawal.[[256]](#cite_note-georgiou-256)

### Domestic policy

#### Constitutional policy

Traditionally the Conservative Party has supported the uncodified [constitution of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitution of the United Kingdom") and its traditional [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") of [politics](/wiki/Politics_of_the_United_Kingdom "Politics of the United Kingdom"). The party opposed many of [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair")'s reforms, such as the removal of the hereditary peers,[[258]](#cite_note-258) the incorporation of the [European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/European_Convention_on_Human_Rights "European Convention on Human Rights") into British law, and the 2009 creation of the [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom"), a function formerly [carried out by the House of Lords](/wiki/Lords_of_Appeal_in_Ordinary "Lords of Appeal in Ordinary").

There was also a split on whether to introduce a British Bill of Rights that would replace the [Human Rights Act 1998](/wiki/Human_Rights_Act_1998 "Human Rights Act 1998"); David Cameron expressed support, but party [grandee](/wiki/Grandee "Grandee") Ken Clarke described it as "xenophobic and legal nonsense".[[259]](#cite_note-259)

In 2019 the Conservatives' manifesto committed to a broad constitutional review in a line which read "after Brexit we also need to look at the broader aspects of our constitution: the relationship between the government, parliament and the courts".[[260]](#cite_note-260)

#### Justice, crime and security policy

In 2010 the Conservatives campaigned to cut the perceived bureaucracy of the modern police force and pledged greater legal protection to people convicted of defending themselves against intruders.

The party has also campaigned for the creation of a UK Bill of Rights to replace the [Human Rights Act 1998](/wiki/Human_Rights_Act_1998 "Human Rights Act 1998"), but this was vetoed by their coalition partners the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)").[[261]](#cite_note-261) The Conservatives' 2017 manifesto pledged to create a national infrastructure police force, subsuming the existing [British Transport Police](/wiki/British_Transport_Police "British Transport Police"); [Civil Nuclear Constabulary](/wiki/Civil_Nuclear_Constabulary "Civil Nuclear Constabulary"); and [Ministry of Defence Police](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_Police "Ministry of Defence Police") "to improve the protection of critical infrastructure such as nuclear sites, railways and the strategic road network".[[262]](#cite_note-262)

#### Education and research

In education, the Conservatives pledged to review the [National Curriculum](/wiki/National_Curriculum "National Curriculum"), introduce the English Baccalaureate, and reform [GCSE](/wiki/GCSE "GCSE"), [A-level](/wiki/A-level "A-level"), other national qualifications, apprenticeships and training.[[263]](#cite_note-263) The restoration of discipline was also highlighted, as they want it to be easier for pupils to be searched for contraband items, the granting of anonymity to teachers accused by pupils, and the banning of expelled pupils being returned to schools via appeal panels.

In higher education, the Conservatives have increased tuition fees to £9,250 per year, however have ensured that this will not be paid by anyone until they are earning over £25,000. The Scottish Conservatives also support the re-introduction of tuition fees in Scotland. In 2016 the Conservative government extended student loan access in England to postgraduate students to help improve access to education.[[264]](#cite_note-264)

Within the EU, the UK was one of the largest recipients of research funding in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), receiving £7 billion between 2007 and 2015, which is invested in universities and research-intensive businesses.[[265]](#cite_note-265) Following the vote to leave the EU, Prime Minister [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") guaranteed that the Conservative government would protect funding for existing research and development projects in the UK.[[266]](#cite_note-266) In 2017, the Conservatives introduced the [T Level](/wiki/T_Level "T Level") qualification aimed at improving the teaching and administration of technical education.[[267]](#cite_note-267)

## Organisation

### Party structure

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Conservative_Party_conference_2011.jpg/250px-Conservative_Party_conference_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Conservative_Party_conference_2011.jpg)

The [National Conservative Convention](/wiki/National_Conservative_Convention "National Conservative Convention") is held during the [Conservative Party Conference](/wiki/Conservative_Party_Conference "Conservative Party Conference").

The Conservative Party comprises the voluntary party, parliamentary party (sometimes called the political party) and the professional party.

Members of the public join the party by becoming part of a local constituency [Conservative Association](/wiki/Conservative_Association "Conservative Association").[[268]](#cite_note-Central-Office-v-Burrell-268) The country is also divided into regions, with each region containing a number of areas, both having a similar structure to constituency associations. The [National Conservative Convention](/wiki/National_Conservative_Convention "National Conservative Convention") sets the voluntary party's direction. It is composed of all association chairs, officers from areas and regions, and 42 representatives and the Conservative Women's Organisation.[[269]](#cite_note-organisation-269) The Convention meets twice a year. Its Annual General Meeting is usually held at Spring Forum, with another meeting usually held at the [Conservative Party Conference](/wiki/Conservative_Party_Conference "Conservative Party Conference"). In the organisation of the Conservative Party, constituency associations dominate selection of local candidates, and some associations have organised [open](/wiki/Open_primary "Open primary") [parliamentary primaries](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK)_parliamentary_primaries "Conservative Party (UK) parliamentary primaries").

The [1922 Committee](/wiki/1922_Committee "1922 Committee") consists of [backbench](/wiki/Backbench "Backbench") MPs, meeting weekly while parliament is sitting. Frontbench MPs have an open invitation to attend. The 1922 Committee plays a crucial role in the selection of party leaders. All Conservative MPs are members of the 1922 Committee by default. There are 20 executive members of the committee, agreed by consensus among backbench MPs.

The [Conservative Campaign Headquarters](/wiki/Conservative_Campaign_Headquarters "Conservative Campaign Headquarters") (CCHQ) is effectively head of the Professional Party and leads [financing](/wiki/Political_funding_in_the_United_Kingdom "Political funding in the United Kingdom"), organisation of elections and drafting of policy.

The [Conservative Party Board](/wiki/Conservative_Party_Board "Conservative Party Board") is the party's ultimate [decision-making](/wiki/Decision-making "Decision-making") body, responsible for all operational matters (including fundraising, membership and candidates) and is made up of representatives from each (voluntary, political and professional) section of the Party.[[269]](#cite_note-organisation-269) The Party Board meets about once a month and works closely with CCHQ, elected representatives and the voluntary membership mainly through a number of management sub-committees (such as membership, candidates and conferences).

### Leadership

The leader of the Conservative Party has been [Kemi Badenoch](/wiki/Kemi_Badenoch "Kemi Badenoch") since 2 November 2024.

The leader of a party that wins an election invariably becomes Prime Minister. The political task of the leader of the Conservative Party is, if possible, to win a [general election](/wiki/General_election "General election") for, or at least to secure the survival of, the Conservative and Unionist party.[[270]](#cite_note-hill0625-270) William Hague has described the Tory Party leadership selection process as "absolute monarchy moderated by regicide".[[270]](#cite_note-hill0625-270)

Historically the leader of the Conservative Party was chosen by a "Magic Circle" of senior figures from which, after a "customary processes of consultation" within the Party, an opaque process led to a new leader "emerging".[[271]](#cite_note-gimson-271) In 1957 [Anthony Eden](/wiki/Anthony_Eden "Anthony Eden") resigned, and was expected to be succeeded by [Rab Butler](/wiki/Rab_Butler "Rab Butler"), who was expected to win, or [Harold Macmillan](/wiki/Harold_Macmillan "Harold Macmillan"). Unexpectedly [Alec Douglas-Home](/wiki/Alec_Douglas-Home "Alec Douglas-Home") was chosen. [Iain Macleod](/wiki/Iain_Macleod "Iain Macleod") in *[The Spectator](/wiki/The_Spectator "The Spectator")* suggested that the leadership race had been fixed by the "Magic Circle" of nine senior Conservatives, eight out of whom were [Old Etonians](/wiki/Old_Etonian "Old Etonian").[[272]](#cite_note-272)

Douglas-Home saw the need for democratisation, and in the mid-1960s instituted a new mechanism for selecting a leader: they would be elected by Conservative MPs in a process presided over by the Chairman of the [1922 Committee](/wiki/1922_Committee "1922 Committee"). To stand, an MP needed a proposer and a seconder. After a first ballot, a candidate with both an overall majority and 15% more of the votes cast than any other candidate would become leader. Otherwise there would be a second ballot, with the winner of an overall majority becoming leader. If still no winner, the three top candidates would take part in a final ballot, with MPs voting for their first and second preferences. [Edward Heath](/wiki/Edward_Heath "Edward Heath") won the first election under these rules.

In 1998, when [William Hague](/wiki/William_Hague "William Hague") was party leader, he introduced reforms intended to make the election more democratic. MPs would vote repeatedly to whittle the contenders down to two, one of whom would be picked by a vote of the entire party membership. These rules, current as of 2025, are explained in detail in a House of Commons research briefing, with brief description of earlier procedures.[[273]](#cite_note-273) Once a leader has been elected, they cannot be challenged for the leadership for one year[[270]](#cite_note-hill0625-270) (although they can be pressed to resign).

### Membership

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/UK_popular_vote.svg/500px-UK_popular_vote.svg.png)](/wiki/File:UK_popular_vote.svg)

Share of the vote received by Conservatives (blue), Whigs/Liberals/Liberal Democrats (orange), Labour (red) and others (grey) in general elections since 1832[[274]](#cite_note-274)[[275]](#cite_note-275)

Membership peaked in the mid-1950s at approximately 3 million, before declining steadily through the second half of the 20th century.[[276]](#cite_note-hoclibrary-276) Despite an initial boost shortly after [David Cameron's election as leader](/wiki/2005_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "2005 Conservative Party leadership election") in December 2005, membership resumed its decline in 2006 to a lower level than when he was elected. In 2010 the Conservative Party had about 177,000 members according to activist [Tim Montgomerie](/wiki/Tim_Montgomerie "Tim Montgomerie"),[[277]](#cite_note-277) and in 2013 membership was estimated by the party itself at 134,000.[[278]](#cite_note-278) The Conservative Party had a membership of 124,000 in March 2018.[[279]](#cite_note-279) In May 2019 its membership was thought to be around 160,000, with over half of its members being over 55.[[280]](#cite_note-280)[[281]](#cite_note-281) Its membership rose to 200,000 in March 2021.[[282]](#cite_note-282) In July 2022 it had 172,437 members.[[283]](#cite_note-July_2022_membership-283) In July 2025, it was revealed that the party's membership had fallen to 123,000.[[2]](#cite_note-Membership_report,_July_2025-2)

### Prospective parliamentary candidates

Further information: [Prospective parliamentary candidate](/wiki/Prospective_parliamentary_candidate "Prospective parliamentary candidate")

Associations select their constituency's candidates.[[268]](#cite_note-Central-Office-v-Burrell-268)[[284]](#cite_note-dummies-284) Some associations [have organised open parliamentary primaries](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK)_parliamentary_primaries "Conservative Party (UK) parliamentary primaries"). A constituency Association must choose a candidate using the rules approved by, and (in England, Wales and Northern Ireland) from a list established by, the Committee on Candidates of the [Board of the Conservative Party](/wiki/Board_of_the_Conservative_Party "Board of the Conservative Party").[[285]](#cite_note-285) Prospective candidates apply to the [Conservative Central Office](/wiki/Conservative_Central_Office "Conservative Central Office") to be included on the approved list of candidates, some candidates will be given the option of applying for any seat they choose, while others may be restricted to certain constituencies.[[286]](#cite_note-personal-representation-286)[[287]](#cite_note-costly-287) A Conservative MP can only be deselected at a special general meeting of the local Conservative association, which can only be organised if backed by a petition of more than fifty members.[[286]](#cite_note-personal-representation-286)

### Young Conservatives

Main article: [Young Conservatives (UK)](/wiki/Young_Conservatives_(UK) "Young Conservatives (UK)")

[Young Conservatives](/wiki/Young_Conservatives_(UK) "Young Conservatives (UK)") is the [youth wing](/wiki/Youth_wing "Youth wing") of the Conservative Party for members aged 25 and under. The organisation aims to increase youth ownership and engagement in local associations.[[288]](#cite_note-telegraph16/03/2018-288) From 1998 to 2015, the youth wing was called [Conservative Future](/wiki/Conservative_Future "Conservative Future"), and had branches at universities and at parliamentary constituency level. It was shut down in 2015 after allegations that bullying by [Mark Clarke](/wiki/Mark_Clarke_(politician) "Mark Clarke (politician)") had caused the suicide of Elliot Johnson, a 21-year-old party activist.[[289]](#cite_note-289)[[290]](#cite_note-290)[[288]](#cite_note-telegraph16/03/2018-288) The current incarnation was launched in March 2018.

### Conferences

The major annual party events are the Spring Forum and the [Conservative Party Conference](/wiki/Conservative_Party_Conference "Conservative Party Conference"), which takes place in Autumn in alternately Manchester or Birmingham. This is when the [National Conservative Convention](/wiki/National_Conservative_Convention "National Conservative Convention") holds meetings.

### Funding

In the first decade of the twenty-first century, half the [party's funding](/wiki/Party_funding "Party funding") came from a cluster of fifty "donor groups", and a third of it from only fifteen.[[291]](#cite_note-291) In the year after the 2010 general election, half the Conservatives' funding came from the financial sector.[[292]](#cite_note-292)

For 2013 the Conservative Party had an income of £25.4 million, of which £749,000 came from membership subscriptions.[[293]](#cite_note-293) In 2015, according to accounts filed with the [Electoral Commission](/wiki/Electoral_Commission_(United_Kingdom) "Electoral Commission (United Kingdom)"), the party had an income of about £41.8 million and expenditures of about £41 million.[[294]](#cite_note-294)

Construction businesses, including the [Wates Group](/wiki/Wates_Group "Wates Group") and [JCB](/wiki/JCB_(heavy_equipment_manufacturer) "JCB (heavy equipment manufacturer)"), have also been significant donors to the party, contributing £430,000 and £8.1m respectively between 2007 and 2017.[[295]](#cite_note-TCI-25Aug2017-295)

The [Advisory Board](/wiki/Advisory_Board_(Conservative_Party) "Advisory Board (Conservative Party)") of the party represents donors who have given significant sums to the party, typically in excess of £250,000.[[296]](#cite_note-TimesFeb22-296)

In December 2022 *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* reported 10% of Conservative peers were large party donors and gave nearly £50m in total. 27 out of the party's 274 peers had given over £100,000 to the Conservatives. At least 6 large donor peers got government jobs in the 10 years to 2022.[[297]](#cite_note-guardian29/12/2022-297)

#### Financial ties to Russian oligarchs

The Conservative Party has received funding from [Russian oligarchs](/wiki/Russian_oligarchs "Russian oligarchs"), beginning in the early 2000s, for which it has been criticised.[[298]](#cite_note-NYTBradley-298)[[299]](#cite_note-299) Scrutiny became more prominent after [alleged interference](/wiki/Russian_interference_in_the_2016_Brexit_referendum "Russian interference in the 2016 Brexit referendum") in the [2016 Brexit referendum](/wiki/2016_Brexit_referendum "2016 Brexit referendum") by the Kremlin to support the [Vote Leave campaign](/wiki/Vote_Leave "Vote Leave"), and increased after the *[Intelligence and Security Committee Russia](/wiki/Intelligence_and_Security_Committee_Russia_report "Intelligence and Security Committee Russia report")* report into [Russian interference in British politics](/wiki/Russian_interference_in_British_politics "Russian interference in British politics") was published in July 2020. Concerns over Conservative Party funds have become increasingly controversial due to Vladimir Putin's human rights abuses and the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[300]](#cite_note-PoliticoWebber-300)

One of the first was Lubov Chernukhin, wife of former deputy finance minister and investment company [VEB.RF](/wiki/VEB.RF "VEB.RF") founder [Vladmir Chernukhin](/wiki/Vladimir_Chernukhin "Vladimir Chernukhin"), who had donated north of £2.2 million as of the start of the Russian invasion of Ukraine.[[301]](#cite_note-301)[[302]](#cite_note-FinCEN_Files_reporting_team_2020_i036-302) Donations to British political parties is only legal for citizens; the individuals who donated to the party had dual UK-Russian citizenship, and the donations were legal and properly declared.[[303]](#cite_note-Greenwood_Midolo_O'Neill_Fisher_2020_m974-303) However, an investigation conducted by *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* shortly after the invasion of Ukraine determined that a £399,810 donation made by British-Israeli businessman [Ehud Sheleg](/wiki/Ehud_Sheleg "Ehud Sheleg") in 2018 was in fact given directly to him by his father-in-law, Russian oligarch [Sergei Kopytov](/wiki/Sergei_Kopylov "Sergei Kopylov"). Kopytov, a former minister in Russian-occupied Crimea, has strong ties to Vladimir Putin's government.[[298]](#cite_note-NYTBradley-298) [Barclays Bank](/wiki/Barclays_Bank "Barclays Bank") reported that in January 2021, they "[traced] a clear line back from this donation to its ultimate source", and reported it accordingly to the [National Crime Agency](/wiki/National_Crime_Agency "National Crime Agency").[[298]](#cite_note-NYTBradley-298)

An investigation by the [Good Law Project](/wiki/Good_Law_Project "Good Law Project") found that in spite of Johnson's claims that donations from those with links to the Russian government was to stop,[[300]](#cite_note-PoliticoWebber-300) since the start of the war, the Conservatives have accepted at least £243,000 from Russia and Kremlin-associated donors.[[304]](#cite_note-304) In February 2022, the Labour Party used Electoral Commission information to calculate that donors who had made money from Russia or Russians had given £1.93m to either the Conservative party or constituency associations since [Boris Johnson's premiership](/wiki/Premiership_of_Boris_Johnson "Premiership of Boris Johnson") began.[[305]](#cite_note-305) Then-party leader Liz Truss said that the donations would not be returned, stating that they had been "properly declared".[[306]](#cite_note-Gutteridge_2022_c850-306)

### International affiliation

The Conservative Party is a member of a number of international organisations, most notably the [International Democracy Union](/wiki/International_Democracy_Union "International Democracy Union") which unites right-wing parties including the United States [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), the [Liberal Party of Australia](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Australia "Liberal Party of Australia"), the [Conservative Party of Canada](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Conservative Party of Canada") and the South Korean [People Power Party](/wiki/People_Power_Party_(South_Korea) "People Power Party (South Korea)").

At a European level, the Conservatives were members of the [European Conservatives and Reformists Party](/wiki/European_Conservatives_and_Reformists_Party "European Conservatives and Reformists Party") (ECR Party), which unites conservative parties in opposition to a federal [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), through which the Conservatives have ties to the [Ulster Unionist Party](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party") and the governing parties of Israel and Turkey, [Likud](/wiki/Likud "Likud") and the [Justice and Development Party](/wiki/Justice_and_Development_Party_(Turkey) "Justice and Development Party (Turkey)") respectively. In the European Parliament, the Conservative Party's MEPs sat in the [European Conservatives and Reformists](/wiki/European_Conservatives_and_Reformists_Group "European Conservatives and Reformists Group") Group (ECR Group), which is affiliated to the ACRE. Party leader David Cameron pushed the foundation of the ECR, which was launched in 2009, along with the Czech [Civic Democratic Party](/wiki/Civic_Democratic_Party_(Czech_Republic) "Civic Democratic Party (Czech Republic)") and the Polish [Law and Justice](/wiki/Law_and_Justice "Law and Justice"), before which the Conservative Party's MEPs sat in the [European Democrats](/wiki/European_Democrats "European Democrats"), which had become a subgroup of the [European People's Party](/wiki/European_People%27s_Party_(European_Parliament_group) "European People's Party (European Parliament group)") in the 1990s. Since the [2014 European election](/wiki/2014_European_election "2014 European election"), the ECR Group has been the third-largest group, with the largest members being the Conservatives (nineteen MEPs), Law and Justice (eighteen MEPs), the [Liberal Conservative Reformers](/wiki/Liberal_Conservative_Reformers "Liberal Conservative Reformers") (five MEPs), and the [Danish People's Party](/wiki/Danish_People%27s_Party "Danish People's Party") and [New Flemish Alliance](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance") (four MEPs each). In June 2009 the Conservatives required a further four partners apart from the Polish and Czech supports to qualify for official [fraction](/wiki/Political_groups_of_the_European_Parliament "Political groups of the European Parliament") status in the parliament; the rules state that a European parliamentary caucus requires at least 25 [MEPs](/wiki/MEPs "MEPs") from at least seven of the 27 [EU member states](/wiki/EU_member_states "EU member states").[[307]](#cite_note-guardian_03062009-307) In forming the caucus, the party broke with two decades of co-operation by the UK's Conservative Party with the mainstream European Christian Democrats and conservatives in the European parliament, the [European People's Party](/wiki/European_People%27s_Party "European People's Party") (EPP). It did so on the grounds that it is dominated by European [federalists](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism") and supporters of the [Lisbon treaty](/wiki/Lisbon_treaty "Lisbon treaty"), which the Conservatives were generally highly critical of.[[307]](#cite_note-guardian_03062009-307)

### Logo

When Sir Christopher Lawson was appointed as a marketing director at [Conservative Central Office](/wiki/Conservative_Central_Office "Conservative Central Office") in 1981, he developed a logo design based on the [Olympic flame](/wiki/Olympic_flame "Olympic flame") in the colours of the [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack"),[[308]](#cite_note-Bruce1992-308) which was intended to represent leadership, striving to win, dedication, and a sense of community.[[309]](#cite_note-309) The emblem was adopted for the 1983 general election.[[308]](#cite_note-Bruce1992-308) In 1989, the party's director of communications, Brendan Bruce, found through [market research](/wiki/Market_research "Market research") that recognition of the symbol was low and that people found it old fashioned and uninspiring. Using a design company headed by [Michael Peters](/wiki/Michael_Peters_(designer) "Michael Peters (designer)"), an image of a hand carrying a torch was developed, which referenced the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty").[[310]](#cite_note-310)

In 2006, there was a rebranding exercise to emphasise the Conservatives' commitment to environmentalism; a project costing £40,000 resulted in a sketched silhouette of an [oak tree](/wiki/Quercus_robur "Quercus robur"), a national symbol, which was said to represent "strength, endurance, renewal and growth".[[311]](#cite_note-311) A change from green to the traditional Conservative blue colour appeared in 2007,[[312]](#cite_note-312) followed by a version with the Union Jack superimposed in 2010.[[313]](#cite_note-313) An alternative version featuring the colours of the [Rainbow flag](/wiki/Rainbow_flag_(LGBT) "Rainbow flag (LGBT)") was unveiled for an [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") event at the 2009 conference in Manchester.[[314]](#cite_note-314)

## Party factions

The Conservative Party has a variety of internal factions or ideologies, including [one-nation conservatism](/wiki/One-nation_conservatism "One-nation conservatism"),[[317]](#cite_note-new_identity-317)[[318]](#cite_note-318) [Christian democracy](/wiki/Christian_democracy "Christian democracy"),[[319]](#cite_note-319) [social conservatism](/wiki/Cornerstone_Group "Cornerstone Group"), [Thatcherism](/wiki/Thatcherism "Thatcherism"), [traditional conservatism](/wiki/Traditional_conservatism "Traditional conservatism"), [neoconservatism](/wiki/British_neoconservatism "British neoconservatism"),[[320]](#cite_note-320)[[321]](#cite_note-321) [Euroscepticism](/wiki/Euroscepticism "Euroscepticism"),[[322]](#cite_note-whitaker-322) and, since 2016, [right-wing populism](/wiki/Right-wing_populism "Right-wing populism").[[19]](#cite_note-Ba23-19)[[323]](#cite_note-Windsor_2022_p732-323)

### One-nation Conservatives

|  |
| --- |
| Part of [a series](/wiki/Category:One-nation_conservatism "Category:One-nation conservatism") on |

[One-nation conservatism](/wiki/One-nation_conservatism "One-nation conservatism") was the party's dominant ideology in the 20th century until the rise of [Thatcherism](/wiki/Thatcherism "Thatcherism") in the 1970s. It has included in its ranks Conservative Prime Ministers such as [Stanley Baldwin](/wiki/Stanley_Baldwin "Stanley Baldwin"), [Harold Macmillan](/wiki/Harold_Macmillan "Harold Macmillan") and [Edward Heath](/wiki/Edward_Heath "Edward Heath").[[324]](#cite_note-324) One-nation Conservatives in the contemporary party include former [First Secretary of State](/wiki/First_Secretary_of_State "First Secretary of State") [Damian Green](/wiki/Damian_Green "Damian Green"), the current chair of the [One Nation Conservatives](/wiki/One_Nation_Conservatives_(caucus) "One Nation Conservatives (caucus)") caucus.

The name itself comes from a famous phrase of [Disraeli](/wiki/Disraeli "Disraeli"). Ideologically, one-nation conservatism identifies itself with a broad [paternalistic conservative](/wiki/Paternalistic_conservative "Paternalistic conservative") stance. One-nation Conservatives are often associated with the [Tory Reform Group](/wiki/Tory_Reform_Group "Tory Reform Group") and the [Bow Group](/wiki/Bow_Group "Bow Group"). Adherents believe in [social cohesion](/wiki/Social_cohesion "Social cohesion") and support social institutions that maintain harmony between different interest groups, classes, and—more recently—different races or religions. These institutions have typically included the [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state"), the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), and local government.

One-nation Conservatives often invoke [Edmund Burke](/wiki/Edmund_Burke "Edmund Burke") and his emphasis on [civil society](/wiki/Civil_society "Civil society") ("little platoons") as the foundations of society, as well as his opposition to radical politics of all types. The Red Tory theory of [Phillip Blond](/wiki/Phillip_Blond "Phillip Blond") is a strand of the one-nation school of thought; prominent Red Tories include former Cabinet Ministers [Iain Duncan Smith](/wiki/Iain_Duncan_Smith "Iain Duncan Smith") and [Eric Pickles](/wiki/Eric_Pickles "Eric Pickles") and [Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State](/wiki/Parliamentary_Under-Secretary_of_State "Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State") [Jesse Norman](/wiki/Jesse_Norman "Jesse Norman").[[325]](#cite_note-325) There is a difference of opinion among supporters regarding the European Union. Some support it perhaps stemming from an extension of the cohesion principle to the international level, though others are strongly against the EU (such as [Peter Tapsell](/wiki/Peter_Tapsell_(UK_politician) "Peter Tapsell (UK politician)")).

### Free-market Conservatives

The "free-market wing" of [economic liberals](/wiki/Economic_liberals "Economic liberals") achieved dominance after the election of Margaret Thatcher as party leader in 1975. Their goal was to reduce the role of the government in the economy and to this end, they supported cuts in direct taxation, the [privatisation](/wiki/Privatisation "Privatisation") of [nationalised](/wiki/Nationalised "Nationalised") industries and a reduction in the size and scope of the welfare state. Supporters of the "free-market wing" have been labelled as "[Thatcherites](/wiki/Thatcherites "Thatcherites")". The group has disparate views of social policy: Thatcher herself was [socially conservative](/wiki/Socially_conservative "Socially conservative") and a practising [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican"), but the free-market wing in the Conservative Party harbours a range of social opinions from the [civil libertarian](/wiki/Civil_libertarian "Civil libertarian") views of [Michael Portillo](/wiki/Michael_Portillo "Michael Portillo"), [Daniel Hannan](/wiki/Daniel_Hannan "Daniel Hannan"), and [David Davis](/wiki/David_Davis_(British_politician) "David Davis (British politician)") to the [traditional conservatism](/wiki/Traditional_conservatism "Traditional conservatism") of former party leaders [William Hague](/wiki/William_Hague "William Hague") and [Iain Duncan Smith](/wiki/Iain_Duncan_Smith "Iain Duncan Smith"). The Thatcherite wing is also associated with the concept of a "classless society".[[326]](#cite_note-326)

Whilst a number of party members are [pro-European](/wiki/Pro-European "Pro-European"), some free-marketeers are [Eurosceptic](/wiki/Eurosceptic "Eurosceptic"), perceiving most EU regulations as interference in the free market and/or a threat to British sovereignty. EU centralisation also conflicts with the localist ideals that have grown in prominence within the party in recent years. Rare Thatcherite Europhiles included [Leon Brittan](/wiki/Leon_Brittan "Leon Brittan"). Many take inspiration from Thatcher's Bruges speech in 1988, in which she declared that "we have not successfully rolled back the frontiers of the state in Britain only to see them reimposed at a European level". A number of free-market Conservatives have signed the [Better Off Out](/wiki/Better_Off_Out "Better Off Out") pledge to leave the EU.[[327]](#cite_note-327) Thatcherites and [economic liberals](/wiki/Economic_liberals "Economic liberals") in the party tend to support [Atlanticism](/wiki/Atlanticism "Atlanticism"), something exhibited between Margaret Thatcher and [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan").

Thatcher herself claimed philosophical inspiration from the works of Burke and [Friedrich Hayek](/wiki/Friedrich_Hayek "Friedrich Hayek") for her defence of liberal economics. Groups associated with this tradition include the [No Turning Back](/wiki/No_Turning_Back_(political_group) "No Turning Back (political group)") Group and [Conservative Way Forward](/wiki/Conservative_Way_Forward "Conservative Way Forward"), whilst Enoch Powell and [Keith Joseph](/wiki/Keith_Joseph "Keith Joseph") are usually cited as early influences in the movement.[[328]](#cite_note-328) Some free-market supporters and [Christian Democrats](/wiki/Christian_Democrats "Christian Democrats") within the party tend to advocate the [Social Market Economy](/wiki/Social_Market_Economy "Social Market Economy"), which supports free markets alongside social and environmental responsibility, as well a welfare state. Joseph was the first to introduce the model idea into British politics, writing the publication: *Why Britain needs a Social Market Economy*.

### Traditionalist Conservatives

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(February 2026)* |

This [socially conservative](/wiki/Socially_conservative "Socially conservative") right-wing grouping is currently associated with the [Cornerstone Group](/wiki/Cornerstone_Group "Cornerstone Group") (or Faith, Flag and Family), and is the oldest tradition within the Conservative Party, closely associated with [High Toryism](/wiki/High_Toryism "High Toryism"). The name stems from its support for three social institutions: the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), the [unitary British state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") and the family. To this end, it emphasises the country's [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") heritage, oppose any transfer of power away from the United Kingdom—either downwards to the nations and regions or upwards to the European Union—and seek to place greater emphasis on [traditional family](/wiki/Traditional_family "Traditional family") structures to repair what it sees as a broken society in the UK. It is a strong advocate of marriage and believes the Conservative Party should back the institution with tax breaks and have opposed the alleged assaults on both traditional family structures and fatherhood.[[329]](#cite_note-329)

Prominent MPs from this wing of the party include [Andrew Rosindell](/wiki/Andrew_Rosindell "Andrew Rosindell"), [Edward Leigh](/wiki/Edward_Leigh "Edward Leigh") and [Jacob Rees-Mogg](/wiki/Jacob_Rees-Mogg "Jacob Rees-Mogg")—the latter two being prominent Roman Catholics, notable in a faction marked out by its support for the established Church of England.

### Relationships between the factions

Sometimes two groupings have united to oppose the third. Both Thatcherite and traditionalist Conservatives rebelled over Europe (and in particular Maastricht) during [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major")'s premiership; and traditionalist and One Nation MPs united to inflict Margaret Thatcher's only major defeat in Parliament, over Sunday trading.

Not all Conservative MPs can be easily placed within one of the above groupings. For example, John Major was the ostensibly "Thatcherite" candidate during the [1990 leadership election](/wiki/1990_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "1990 Conservative Party leadership election"), but he consistently promoted One-Nation Conservatives to the higher reaches of his cabinet during his time as prime minister. These included [Kenneth Clarke](/wiki/Kenneth_Clarke "Kenneth Clarke") as Chancellor of the Exchequer and [Michael Heseltine](/wiki/Michael_Heseltine "Michael Heseltine") as deputy prime minister.[[330]](#cite_note-330)

## Election results and campaigns

National campaigning within the Conservative Party is fundamentally managed by [the CCHQ campaigning team](/wiki/Conservative_Campaign_Headquarters#Campaigning "Conservative Campaign Headquarters"), which is part of its central office. However, it also delegates local responsibility to Conservative associations in the area, usually to a team of Conservative activists and volunteers[[269]](#cite_note-organisation-269) in that area, but campaigns are still deployed from and thus managed by [CCHQ](/wiki/CCHQ "CCHQ")
National campaigning sometimes occurs in-house by volunteers and staff at [CCHQ](/wiki/CCHQ "CCHQ") in [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster "Westminster").[[331]](#cite_note-331)

The *Voter Communications Department* is line-managed by the Conservative Director of Communications who upholds overall responsibility, though she has many staff supporting her, and the whole of CCHQ at election time, her department being one of the most predominant at this time, including project managers, executive assistants, politicians, and volunteers.[[332]](#cite_note-332) The Conservative Party also has regional call centres and VoteSource do-it-from-home accounts.

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-342)** The first election held under the [Reform Act 1867](/wiki/Reform_Act_1867 "Reform Act 1867").
2. **[^](#cite_ref-344)** The first election held under the [Representation of the People Act 1884](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1884 "Representation of the People Act 1884") and the [Redistribution of Seats Act 1885](/wiki/Redistribution_of_Seats_Act_1885 "Redistribution of Seats Act 1885").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-349)** The first election held under the [Representation of the People Act 1918](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1918 "Representation of the People Act 1918") in which all men over 21, and most women over the age of 30 could vote, and therefore a much larger electorate.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-351)** The first election held under the [Representation of the People Act 1928](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1928 "Representation of the People Act 1928") which gave all women aged over 21 the vote.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-352)** Franchise extended to all 18- to 20-year-olds under the [Representation of the People Act 1969](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1969 "Representation of the People Act 1969").
